title: Sh. Kapil Dev Shah vs Cpwd Through Executive Engineer on 21 April, 2012
body: Sh. Kapil Dev Shah vs Cpwd Through Executive Engineer on 21 April, 2012 1 IN THE COURT OF MS. VEENA RANI : COMMERCIAL CIVIL JUDGE; NEW DELHI DISTRICT : NEW DELHI. Suit No:45/2011 Sh. Kapil Dev Shah .....Plaintiff Versus 1. CPWD through Executive Engineer 2. New Delhi Municipal Council through its Secretary .....Defendants ORDER 1. This order shall dispose of an application of the applicant/plaintiff u/o 39 rule 1 and 2 r/w Section 151 CPC for interim injunction. Vide this application plaintiff is seeking ad­interim injunction against the defendants, their agents or representative restraining them from demolishing, removing or creating any obstructions in suit property till the final disposal of the present suit. 2. Brief facts, as stated by the plaintiff are that he is a law abiding senior citizen and residing in his Jhuggi/suit property bearing No:D­11/155, West Kidwai Nagar, New Delhi and also having his voter I­card and his BPL Ration Card No:345788. It is the case of the plaintiff that there are about 130 Jhuggis in the locality and only he is being victimized and Suit No:45/2011, Sh. Kapil Dev Shah Vs. CPWD & NDMC 2 harassed by the defendants and defendant No:1 has served a notice u/s 2 of Section 5A of the Public Premises (Eviction of Unauthorized occupants) Act 1971 dt.30­6­2007 against the suit property. It is stated that area of suit property falls under the jurisdiction of defendant No:1 and defendant No:2 has nothing to do with the said area, its development and any other purposes and defendant No:2 is not legally authorized to demolish the suit property without any plan or project etc in its hand. 3. It is averred by the plaintiff that his son Vinod Kumar had filed a writ petition No:8784/2006 in the Hon'ble High Court for installing a PCO and despite repeated directions from the Hon'ble High Court, the defendant No:2 has not complied with the directions of the Hon'ble High Court and they are facing the contempt proceeding in the writ petition No: 13261/2009. It is stated that since the defendant No:2 can not demolish the jhuggi of the plaintiff, therefore, defendant No:2 in connivance with the defendant No:1, in order to punish the plaintiff and his family, served a notice u/s 2 of Section 5A of the Public Premises (Eviction of Unauthorized occupants) Act 1971 dt.30­6­2007 against the suit property in order to pressurize the plaintiff and his family to withdraw the contempt proceedings pending before the Honorable High Court. Suit No:45/2011, Sh. Kapil Dev Shah Vs. CPWD & NDMC 3 4. Written statement filed on behalf of the defendant No:1/ CPWD wherein it is stated that plaintiff is an unauthorized occupant on the government land , therefore, a notice u/s 2 of Section 5A of the Public Premises (Eviction of Unauthorized occupants) Act 1971 dt.30­6­2007 has been served upon the plaintiff in respect of suit property. It is submitted that present suit of the plaintiff is not maintainable as the appropriate remedy is available to the plaintiff by way of an appeal under P.P. Act. It is further stated that being unauthorized occupant of suit property, the plaintiff has no locus standi to file the present suit. Defendant No:1 denied that there are 130 Jhuggis in the locality. It is stated that there is no J.J. Cluster. 5. Defendant No:2/NDMC has also filed written statement wherein it is stated that there is no cause of action against the NDMC, hence present suit is liable to be rejected in view of order VII rule XI of CPC . It is denied by defendant No:2 that there are about 130 jhuggis in locality and only the plaintiff has been victimized and harassed by the defendant. It is stated that proceedings initiated under Public Premises Act, 1971 is a procedure established by law and there is no illegality in the same. It is submitted that no contempt was committed by the answering defendant as alleged by the plaintiff. It is stated that contempt petition of the plaintiff was Suit No:45/2011, Sh. Kapil Dev Shah Vs. CPWD & NDMC 4 dismissed by the hon'ble High Court vide its order dt.20­11­2009. It is denied by the defendant No:2 that , they are acting against the suit property in connivance with the defendant No:1. 6. Plaintiff filed replications to the respective written statement of the defendants and reiterated its averments made in the plaint. 7. I have heard ld counsel for the parties and perused the record carefully. 8. In the case of Gujarat Bottling Co. Ltd. v. Coca Cola Co. reported in [(1995) 5 SCC 545] the Hon'ble Supreme Court, while discussing the factors to be considered by the Courts in exercise of the discretion under order 39 rules 1 & 2 CPC , has observed as follows: "The grant of an interlocutory injunction during the pendency of legal proceedings is a matter requiring the exercise of discretion of the court. While exercising the discretion the court applies the following tests : (i) whether the plaintiff has a prima facie case; (ii) whether the balance of convenience is in favour of the plaintiff; and (iii) whether the plaintiff would suffer an irreparable injury if his Suit No:45/2011, Sh. Kapil Dev Shah Vs. CPWD & NDMC 5 prayer for interlocutory injunction is disallowed. The decision whether or not to grant an interlocutory injunction has to be taken at a time when the existence of the legal right assailed by the plaintiff and its alleged violation are both contested and uncertain and remain uncertain till they are established at the trial on evidence. Relief by way of interlocutory injunction is granted to mitigate the risk of injustice to the plaintiff during the period before that uncertainty could be resolved. The object of the interlocutory injunction is to protect the plaintiff against injury by violation of his right for which he could not be adequately compensated in damages recoverable in the action if the uncertainty were resolved in his favour at the trial. The need for such protection has, however, to be weighed against the corresponding need of the defendant to be protected against injury resulting from his having been prevented from exercising his own legal rights for which he could not be adequately compensated. The court must weigh one need against another and determine where the "balance of convenience" lies. In order to protect the defendant while granting an interlocutory injunction in his favour the court can require the plaintiff to furnish an undertaking so that the defendant can be adequately compensated if the uncertainty were resolved in his favour at the trial." 9. In Dorab Cawasji Warden Vs. Coomi Sorab Warden & Ors. , (1990) 2 SCC 117, this Court, discussing the principles to be kept in mind in considering the prayer for interlocutory mandatory injunction observed : Suit No:45/2011, Sh. Kapil Dev Shah Vs. CPWD & NDMC 6 "The relief of interlocutory mandatory injunctions are thus granted generally to preserve or restore the status quo of the last non­contested status which preceded the pending controversy until the final hearing when full relief may be granted or to compel the undoing of those acts that have been illegally done or the restoration of that which was wrongfully taken from the party complaining. But since the granting of such an injunction to a party who fails or would fail to establish his right at the trial may cause great injustice or irreparable harm to the party against whom it was granted or alternatively not granting of it to a party who succeeds or would succeed may equally cause great injustice or irreparable harm, courts have evolved certain guidelines. 10. In Dalpat Kumar v. Prahlad Singh , (1992)1 SCC 719 : (1992 AIR SCW 3128) a Bench of two Judges (in which K. Ramaswamy, J. was a Member) of this Court held that the phrases "Prima facie case," "balance of convenience" and "irreparable loss" are not rhetoric phrases for incantation but words of width and elasticity, intended to meet myriad situations presented by men's ingenuity in given facts and circumstances and should always be hedged with sound exercise of judicial discretion to meet the ends of justice. The Court would be circumspect before granting the injunction and look to the conduct of the party, the probable injury to either Suit No:45/2011, Sh. Kapil Dev Shah Vs. CPWD & NDMC 7 party and whether the plaintiff could be adequately compensated if injunction is refused. The existence of prima facie right and infraction of the enjoyment of his property or the right is a condition for the grant of temporary injunction. Prima facie case is not to be confused with prima facie title which has to be established on evidence at the trial. Only prima facie case is a substantial question raised, bona fide, which needs investigation and a decision on merits. Satisfaction that there is a prima facie case by itself is not sufficient to grant injunction. The Court further has to satisfy that non­interference by the Court would result in "irreparable injury" to the party seeking relief and that there is no other remedy available to the party except one to grant injunction and he needs protection from the consequences of apprehended injury or dispossession. Irreparable injury, however, does not mean that there must be no physical possibility of repairing the injury but means only that the injury must be a material one, namely one that cannot be adequately compensated by way of damages. The balance of convenience must be in favour of granting injunction. The Court while granting or refusing to grant injunction should exercise sound judicial discretion to find the amount of substantial mischief or injury which is likely to be caused to the parties if the injunction is refused and compare it with that which is likely to be caused to the other side if the injunction is granted. If on weighing competing possibilities or Suit No:45/2011, Sh. Kapil Dev Shah Vs. CPWD & NDMC 8 probabilities of likelihood of injury and if the Court considers that pending the suit, the subject matter should be maintained in status quo, an injunction would be issued. The Court has to exercise its sound judicial discretion in granting or refusing the relief of ad interim injunction pending the suit." 11. In Colgate Palmolive (India) Ltd. Vs. Hindustan Lever Ltd. , AIR 1999 SC 3105, the Hon'ble Supreme Court observed that the other considerations which ought to weigh with the Court hearing the application or petition for the grant of injunctions are as below : (i) Extent of damages being an adequate remedy; (ii) Protect the plaintiff's interest for violation of his rights though however having regard to the injury that may be suffered by the defendants by reason therefor ; (iii) The court while dealing with the matter ought not to ignore the factum of strength of one party's case being stronger than the others; (iv) No fixed rules or notions ought to be had in the matter of grant of injunction but on the facts and circumstances of each case­ the relief being kept flexible; Suit No:45/2011, Sh. Kapil Dev Shah Vs. CPWD & NDMC 9 (v) The issue is to be looked from the point of view as to whether on refusal of the injunction the plaintiff would suffer irreparable loss and injury keeping in view the strength of the parties' case; (vi) Balance of convenience or inconvenience ought to be considered as an important requirement even if there is a serious question or prima facie case in support of the grant; (vii) Whether the grant or refusal of injunction will adversely affect the interest of general public which can or cannot be compensated otherwise. The Prime Facie case: 12. The case of the plaintiff is that he is residing at the suit premises for the last 25 years without any hindrance from defendant no.1 and defendant no.2. In order to show his possession, the plaintiff submitted that he is having a voter I­Card issued by Election Commission and a ration card. It is further submitted that in the vicinity there are about 130 jhuggis and only the plaintiff is being victimized and harassed by the defendant no.1 by serving a notice under Section 2 of Section 5A of the Public Premises Act, 1971. It is also the case of the plaintiff that the NDMC is having jurisdiction over the suit premise. The defendant no.1 has nothing to do with the area and its development and it is also not legally authorized to demolish the suit property. Suit No:45/2011, Sh. Kapil Dev Shah Vs. CPWD & NDMC 10 13. Plaintiff in support of his averments filed certain documents such as copy of the ration card, copy of the election I­Card and copy of the Town Development Department. 14. On the other hand, it is the contention on behalf of the CPWD/defendant no.1 that the plaintiff is an unauthorized occupant on the Government land and the notice under section 2 of the Section 5A of the Public Premises Act, 1971 has been served upon the plaintiff. But the, same has not yet been replied by him. It is also the contention of the defendant no.1 that the plaintiff is not having any locus standi to file the present suit and further that in the vicinity there exist no other jhuggi except of the plaintiff. 15.Certain photographs have also been filed on record of the suit premises showing the suit premises situated near the Government Quarters and at the back side of the garrages. However, from the very first look of the suit premises it can be inferred that this suit property is an unauthorized construction. The plaintiff herein by filing the documents is showing his possession but merely by having the possession can not make the plaintiff an authorised occupant of an unauthorized construction. In order to establish his case the plaintiff has to prima facie show that the suit property Suit No:45/2011, Sh. Kapil Dev Shah Vs. CPWD & NDMC 11 is a legal construction, which he fails to do so, hence, prima facie I am of the view that the plaintiff has not been able to establish a prima facie case in his favour. The balance of convenience. 16. In the present case, the plaintiff is an unauthorized occupant and he cannot exercise his right against an illegal construction therefore, the 'balance of convenience' is also not in his favour at this stage. The Irreparable Loss. 17. In the present case the question is, whether the plaintiff could be adequately compensated in terms of money, if the interim injunction is not granted. The answer appears to be negative, as the issue is more legal than factual. Hence, I am satisfied that the plaintiff would not suffer irreparable loss if the injunction is not granted. 18. Accordingly, the present application under order XXXIX rules 1 and 2 read with Section 151 CPC stands dismissed. However nothing contained herein shall tantamount to have any expression on the merits of the case. Suit No:45/2011, Sh. Kapil Dev Shah Vs. CPWD & NDMC 12 19. Put up for framing of issues, filing of documents and admission- denial, if any, on 18­07­2012. (Announced in open court on 21­4­2012) (VEENA RANI) Commercial Civil Judge: New Delhi District New Delhi. Suit No:45/2011, Sh. Kapil Dev Shah Vs. CPWD & NDMC
